analogy: Think of "Transportation in Animals and Plants" through the chapter's own opening images: LIGHT 123 Light 11 Y ou might have seen a beam of sunlight when it enters a room through a narrow opening or a hole. You may have also seen beams of light from the headlamps of scooters, cars and engines of trains [Fig. Similarly,... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Transportation in Animals and Plants is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that lIGHT 123 Light 11 Y ou might have seen a beam of sunlight when it enters a room through a narrow opening or a hole.

The text explains that you may have also seen beams of light from the headlamps of scooters, cars and engines of trains [Fig.

The text explains that similarly, a beam of light can be seen from a torch.

The text explains that 11.2 Looking at a candle through a straight and a bent pipe you may have seen a beam of searchlight from a light house or from an airport tower [Fig.

The text explains that 11.1 LIGHT T RAVELS ALONG A STRAIGHT LINE Boojho recalls an activity he performed in Class VI.

The text explains that 11.3 Reflection of objects in water at a lighted candle first through a straight pipe and then through a bent pipe (Fig.

The text explains that why was Boojho not able to see the candle flame through a bent pipe?

The text explains that this activity showed that light travels along straight lines.

The text explains that do you know, what happens when light falls on a polished or a shiny surface?

The text explains that 11.2 REFLECTION OF LIGHT One way to change the direction of light is to let it fall on a shiny surface.

The text explains that for example, a shining stainless steel plate or a shining steel spoon can change the direction of light.

The text explains that the surface of water can also act like a mirror and change the path of light.

The text explains that have you ever seen the reflection of trees or buildings in water (Fig.

The text explains that any polished or a shiny surface can act as a mirror.

The text explains that you have learnt in Class VI that a mirror changes the direction of light that falls on it.

The text explains that this change of direction by a mirror is called reflection of light.

The text explains that now direct the beam of light on the mirror from the torch with slits.

The text explains that does the mirror change the direction of light that falls on it?

The text explains that do you find any change in the direction of reflected light?

The text explains that look into the mirror along the direction of the reflected light.

The text explains that be careful, never look directly towards the Sun or its image as it may damage your eyes.

The text explains that the group that scores the maximum will be declared the winner.

The text explains that the object will make movements, such as raising a hand, touching an ear etc.

The text explains that the image will have to make the correct movement following the movement of the object.

The text explains that the rest of the group will watch the movements of the image.

The text explains that if the image fails to make the correct movement, she/he will be retired.

The text explains that another child will take her/his place and the game will continue.

The text explains that archimedes, a Greek scientist, is said to have done just that more than two thousand years ago.

The text explains that when the Romans attacked Syracuse, a coastal city-state in Greece, Archimedes used mirrors arranged as shown in Fig.

The text explains that they were positioned such that they reflected the sunlight on the Roman soldiers.

The text explains that this was an example of triumph of ideas over military might.

Source: NCERT Class 7 Science, Chapter 11: Transportation in Animals and Plants

prelims_traps: Do not treat LIGHT 123 and 11.1 LIGHT T RAVELS ALONG A as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Transportation in Animals and Plants; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Transportation in Animals and Plants through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use LIGHT 123 and 11.1 LIGHT T RAVELS ALONG A to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
